content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

SUPERSEDED
IN PART
by Req.1894S
(see A-98-072)

July 8, 1991

Paul N. "Pete" McCloskey, Jr.
California Foundation on the Environment
and the Economy
909 Montgomery Street, Third Floor
San Francisco, CA 94133

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-263

Dear Mr. McCloskey:

You have requested advice on behalf of the California
Foundation on the Environment and the Economy concerning the new
gift and honoraria limits of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act"). Since you have not named a specific person with
responsibilities under the Act on whose behalf you have requested
this advice, we can only provide the following informal response
to your question.2

Please be aware that the issues raised in this letter present
significant policy questions which will be presented to the
Commission for their consideration in the future as a proposed
regulation. In the meantime, we have provided a conservative and
cautious interpretation of the Act.

QUESTIONS

1. Are payments for travel and accommodations provided to
elected officials and designated employees by the California
Foundation on the Environment and the Economy as part of the
foundation's study-travel program gifts under the Act?

_______________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-263
Page 2


     2.  Under what circumstances would the gift of travel and
accommodations be attributed to sponsors of the event, rather than
the California Foundation on the Environment and the Economy, and
how would such an attribution affect the application of Section
89506?

CONCLUSIONS

     1.  Payments for travel and accommodations provided to
elected officials and designated employees by the California
Foundation on the Environment and the Economy as part of the
foundation's study-travel program would be exempted from the gift
limits of the Act by Section 89506.  The payments would,
however, continue to be disclosable gifts and could result in potential
conflicts of interest.

     2.  The payments for the travel and accommodations would be a
gift from the various sponsors if the sponsors of the conference
know or had reason to know at the time their donations are made
which public officials, or which identifiable group of public
officials, will receive the gifts of travel that the California
Foundation on the Environment and the Economy ultimately provides.
Since the sponsors are not charitable or educational nonprofit
organizations, Section 89506 would not apply.

FACTS

     The California Foundation on the Environment and the Economy
("CFEE") is a nonprofit charitable corporation exempt from
taxation under Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3).  CFEE was
created in 1979 to bring together business, labor, community, and
environmental leaders with public officials, policy makers and
expert academicians, in forums designed to address complex
economic and social issues.

     CFEE generally holds two to three California conferences and
one foreign study-travel project each year.  Various public
officials are invited to each event by CFEE to participate.
CFEE's president, subject to ratification by the Board of
Directors, has sole discretion over the subject matter of its
conferences and study-travel program, where they will take place,
and who will be invited to participate.  CFEE's governing board
consists of 30 members who serve on a voluntary basis.  Only one
member of the board of directors is a public official.

     CFEE finances the various events with an endowment from a
private foundation, and contributions solicited from businesses
(principally corporations), associations, and public agencies
having an interest in the subject matter of the particular seminar
or study-travel project CFEE is holding.  Donors may participate
in the events, at their own expense.

Our File No. A-91-263
Page 3


CFEE has planned a study-travel project to Europe concerning
environmental control technology and alternative energy for
September, 1991. Participants include members of the European
Community, executives and staff of the European Community
Directorates for Environment, Energy, and External Relations. In
addition, CFEE plans to invite a delegation of California
legislators and legislative staff to participate in the European
study-travel program. CFEE plans to pay for travel and
accommodations of the legislators.

ANALYSIS

Section 89504(a) provides, in pertinent part:

     (a) No elected state officer shall accept
gifts from any single source in any calendar year
with a total value of more than two hundred fifty
dollars ($250).

     (b) This section shall not prohibit or limit
the following:

          (1) Payments, advances, or
reimbursements for travel and related lodging
and subsistence permitted by Section 89506.

     Section 89504(b)(1) states that where a public official
receives a gift of travel, Section 89506 controls whether the gift
is subject to the new gift limit. Section 89506 provides:

     (a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements,
for travel, including actual transportation and
related lodging and subsistence which is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose,
or to an issue of state, national, or international
public policy, is not prohibited or limited by this
chapter if either of the following apply:

          (1) The travel is in connection with a
speech given by the elected state officer,
member of a state board or commission, or
designated employee of a state agency, the
lodging and subsistence expenses are limited
to the day immediately preceding, the day of,
and the day immediately following the speech,
and the travel is within the United States.

          (2) The travel is provided by a
government, a governmental agency, a foreign
government, a governmental authority, a bona
fide public or private educational
institution, as defined in Section 203 of the
Revenue and Taxation Code, or a nonprofit
charitable or religious organization which is

Our File No. A-91-263
Page 4


exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3)
of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person
domiciled outside the United States which
substantially satisfies the requirements for
tax exempt status under Section 501(c)(3) of
the Internal Revenue Code.

Section 89506(a), emphasis
added.

It appears that the activity described in your letter is
reasonably related to an issue of state, national, or
international public policy pursuant to Section
89506. You stated that the purpose of the European conference was
to discuss environmental control technology and alternative
energy. Thus while the exception in 89506(a)(1) might
apply to payment for travel to CFEE if the conference was to
be held in the continental United States and the officials were to
make speeches, it will not apply to a conference in Europe where
the officials will not be making speeches.

Section 89506(a)(2), however, provides an additional
exception to the gift and honoraria restrictions of the Act.
For the exception in subdivision (a)(2) to apply, the travel must
be reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose, or
to an issue of state, national, or international public policy and
be paid for by a nonprofit charitable or religious organization
which is exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3).

You stated that CFEE is a tax exempt nonprofit charitable
organization under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.
Thus, where CFEE is the source of the gift, the gifts of travel
that you describe would appear to fall within the exception in
Section 89506(a)(2) and be exempted from the gift and honoraria
limits of the Act. Please note, the payments would still need to
be disclosed and may result in a conflict of interest at a future
time.

However, under some circumstances it is appropriate to pierce
through one organization which appears to be the source of a gift,
to the actual donors of funds used to provide the gift. For
example, in the Bell Advice Letter (Advice Letter No. A-91-077,
copy enclosed), we advised that where an organization was acting
as a mere intermediary for gifts, piercing was appropriate.

According to your facts, CFEE's president, subject to
ratification by the board of directors, has sole discretion over
the subject matter of its conferences and study-travel program,
where they will take place, and who will be invited to
participate. However, CFEE finances the various events with an
endowment from a private foundation, and donations solicited from
businesses, associations, and public agencies having an interest
in the subject matter of the particular seminar or study-travel

Our File No. A-91-263
Page 5


project CFEE is holding.  Donors may also participate in the
events at their own expense.

In the context of contribution limits and disclosure,
piercing through an intermediary was appropriate where the true
source of the funds had provided a contribution to another entity
and designated the candidate who was to receive the contribution.
(Wilson Advice Letter, No. A-79-077, copy attached.)  Under those
circumstances the person acting as the intermediary was not
treated as the source of the contribution.³

Consistent with this reasoning, we conclude that where the
sponsors of the conference know or have reason to know at the time
they make their donations which public officials, or which
identifiable group of public officials will receive the gifts of
travel that CFEE ultimately provides, the sponsors are the true
source of the gift.  For example, if the conference will be on a
topic of which only a few legislators would appropriately attend,
the sponsors will have reason to know who will be invited.
Conversely, where the topic is one that any legislator may be
invited to attend, and there is no past pattern to indicate that a
certain legislator or selective group will be invited, piercing
may not be appropriate.

We do not have sufficient facts to determine whether the
sponsors have reason to know who will be invited.  However, if
they do, the sponsors will be the true source of the gifts to the
public officials, and Section 89506(a)(2) would not exempt the
gifts from the limits of the Act.

I trust this letter has addressed your concerns.  Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
    Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa
Enclosures

_________________________
³  This contrasts with the situation where a person provides a
contribution to a political committee which in turn uses those
funds to make a contribution to a candidate selected solely by the
committee.  Under those circumstances we have said that the
committee has received a contribution from the donor and that the
candidate had received a contribution from the committee.
(Section 85302.)

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission SUPERSEDED IN PART by Req.1894S (see A-98-072) July 8, 1991 Paul N. "Pete" McCloskey, Jr. California Foundation on the Environment and the Economy 909 Montgomery Street, Third Floor San Francisco, CA 94133 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-91-263 Dear Mr. McCloskey: You have requested advice on behalf of the California Foundation on the Environment and the Economy concerning the new gift and honoraria limits of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Since you have not named a specific person with responsibilities under the Act on whose behalf you have requested this advice, we can only provide the following informal response to your question.2 Please be aware that the issues raised in this letter present significant policy questions which will be presented to the Commission for their consideration in the future as a proposed regulation. In the meantime, we have provided a conservative and cautious interpretation of the Act. QUESTIONS 1. Are payments for travel and accommodations provided to elected officials and designated employees by the California Foundation on the Environment and the Economy as part of the foundation's study-travel program gifts under the Act? _______________ 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. A-91-263 Page 2 2. Under what circumstances would the gift of travel and accommodations be attributed to sponsors of the event, rather than the California Foundation on the Environment and the Economy, and how would such an attribution affect the application of Section 89506? CONCLUSIONS 1. Payments for travel and accommodations provided to elected officials and designated employees by the California Foundation on the Environment and the Economy as part of the foundation's study-travel program would be exempted from the gift limits of the Act by Section 89506. The payments would, however, continue to be disclosable gifts and could result in potential conflicts of interest. 2. The payments for the travel and accommodations would be a gift from the various sponsors if the sponsors of the conference know or had reason to know at the time their donations are made which public officials, or which identifiable group of public officials, will receive the gifts of travel that the California Foundation on the Environment and the Economy ultimately provides. Since the sponsors are not charitable or educational nonprofit organizations, Section 89506 would not apply. CFEE was created in 1979 to bring together business, labor, community,
